title: The Virgin Mary — Monthly Feast, St. Januarius of Benevento & St. Alexandra
narrative:
The Ever-Virgin Mary Theotokos, St. Januarius Bishop of Benevento, and St. Alexandra the Martyr

On the twenty-first of Miazia, the monthly feast of the most holy Theotokos falls in the month of the Paschal dawn with a particular radiance: the Virgin who stood at the foot of the Cross in the darkness of Good Friday stands in Miazia's dawning light as the first witness of the Resurrection's meaning, the one who held in her heart through all the darkness the certainty that the promise would be fulfilled. The Ethiopian Church's monthly Marian feast is always a celebration of her continuing intercession, but in Miazia that intercession is specifically Paschal in character: Mary intercedes at the throne of her risen Son, the one whom she carried in her womb, whom she held in her arms at Bethlehem and at the foot of the Cross, and whom she encountered in the garden of the Resurrection as the gardener who called her Son's name with the authority of the eternal Word. The monthly feast of the Theotokos in this month is the Church's most tender act of gratitude for the woman through whom the Resurrection became possible.

Saint Januarius, Bishop of Benevento, is the most celebrated of the Italian martyrs and one of the most persistently miraculous of all the saints venerated in the Western and Oriental traditions alike: the bishop of that southern Italian city who was martyred under Diocletian alongside several of his clergy and deacons, and whose blood, preserved in a reliquary in Naples, has liquefied regularly on his feast day for at least six centuries, an event that continues to be witnessed by enormous crowds and that the tradition receives as the bishop's continuing pastoral presence in his city. The Ethiopian Synaxarium honours Januarius as a figure of the Paschal mystery made permanently visible: the blood that was shed for Christ refusing to dry, remaining perpetually fresh in its vessel, as though the martyrdom had never quite completed itself but remained permanently open to the light of the Resurrection.

Saint Alexandra the Martyr — distinct from the Empress Alexandra of Miazia 14 and from the Queen Alexandra of Miazia 23 — is a confessor whose relics are commemorated on this day in the context of a translation feast: the moving of her remains to a new shrine, and the renewal of her intercessory presence in a new community. The tradition of the translation of relics is one of the Ethiopian church's most ancient practices, and the Miazia illumination receives it with particular richness: the translation of a saint's relics is a small resurrection, a raising-up of the body from the earth where it lay, a movement of the beloved dead through the community of the living. Mary who was assumed body and soul into Heaven, Januarius whose blood remains liquid after sixteen centuries, Alexandra whose bones are carried in procession: three modes of the body's participation in the Paschal mystery. Amen.
